videos.title: THE DARK SIDE OF CUBA THAT IS NOT SHOWN ! - FIGHTING AGAINST HARSH CONDITIONS - HAVANA DOCUMENTARY
videos.description:

In this country documentary, I have provided you with some important information about life in Cuba. I talked about life in Cuba, daily life in Cuba, Cuban market prices, Cuban history, life in the country of Cuba.
 

When you watch the documentary, you will learn interesting information about life in Cuba, Cuban music, Cuban nightlife, Cuban food, Cuba. I also presented information about the climate in Cuba.  Life in Cuba, Cuba and Caribbean culture, Cuba travel, traveling in Cuba, Cuba traditions, rules in Cuba, Cuba life, Cuba laws, Cuba nature, Cuba people, Cuba cities, Cuba capital, Cuba country, Cuba vlog, Cuba house rentals, Cuba citizenship, Cuba culture, Cuba visa, Cuba vlog shooting, Cuba brands, Studying in Cuba, Cuba Erasmus, Religious beliefs in Cuba, Cuba universities, Freedoms in Cuba, Settling in Cuba, Cuba food, Cuba agriculture, Cuba hotels , Cuba natural beauties, Cuba tours, Cuba visa, Cuba places to visit, How Cuba became a country, Cuba trip, Where is Cuba, Cuba people, Cuba job opportunities, Cuba social rights, Cuba labor rights, salaries in Cuba, making a living in Cuba, Cuba banking, why Cuba is poor, Cuba vacation, Cuba minimum wage, Cuba salaries, Cuba religious beliefs, Cuba Muslims, Cuba job opportunities, what kind of place Cuba is, Turks in Cuba, Cuba Turkish immigrants, prohibitions in Cuba, Cuba hospitals, professions that can be done in Cuba, You will watch a fluent Cuba country documentary, Cuba country video on Okan Suvari Media channel about many topics such as Cuba market prices, Cuba education and health system, what kind of place Cuba is, Cuba culture, Cuba summer and winter vacation, Cuba trip, Cuba tickets, Cuba schools, Cuba women, Cuba single women, Cuba men, Cuba living conditions. 

The video will make you feel like a Cuban citizen or you will want to go to Cuba and see the sights. Because Cuba is one of the most interesting countries in the world and the way of life in Cuba is very interesting. Cuban cigars and Cuban coffee are very famous.

videos.transcript: Cuba, a country suspended in time. A choice between what can be said and what can cost your freedom. [Music] [Music] [Music] Most people have heard of Cuba at least once in their lives. It is one of the most populous countries in the Caribbean with around 11 million inhabitants. Its capital city, Havana, has the largest population in Cuba and the Caribbean with 2.5 million inhabitants. The country has struggled with economic difficulties and restrictions for many years. Although Cuba is now well known for tourism, the local people face great difficulties in daily life. They receive low salaries, food is expensive, and they have difficulty accessing medicine. What could be the reason for Cuba's backwardness? The reason is quite obvious. It is political. As a country of revolution and revolutionaries, it is a communist state. This form of government separates Cuba from many other countries that support capitalism. In the past, with the support of the Soviet Union, Cuba pursued policies that opposed superpower countries like the United States. Then when the Soviet Union collapsed, Cuba was crushed by sanctions and embargos from countries like the United States. Consequently, Cuba has poor political and economic relations with many countries today. People live in old houses and drive antique cars. We went there with Christopher, our man in the Caribbean, to investigate the current living conditions in Cuba. He will stay in Havana, Cuba's capital, for 3 days, talk to locals, and show us the side of Cuba not mentioned in the media. We never aim to denigrate Cuba. Rather, we want to give a voice to the people by showing the difficult living conditions there. So, let's begin. On our first day, we landed in Havana on a cloudy morning and made our way through the airport corridors toward the exit. We took a taxi to the city center. Here is one of those antique vehicles associated with Cuba. And here is what the inside looks like. [Music] The average cost to get to the city center by taxi is between $20 and $30. As we drove, we noticed that most of the houses were single or twostory. It was still early in the morning, so there weren't many people around. Cuba, a country suspended in time. From afar, it seems like an island wrapped in color, rhythm, and nostalgia. In here, every block holds a story, a struggle, a choice between what can be said and what can cost your freedom. [Music] We soon arrived at the city center where we met the guide who would help us. First, this old man tried to take us into shops to learn about the food situation and the economy in the capital. We first approached a grocery store, but the woman wasn't interested in talking. After noticing the abundance of money in the guy's wallet, we learned that $1 is officially equivalent to 24 Cuban pesos. Then, we entered a butcher shop and spoke with the butcher for a few minutes. I think [Laughter] [Music] after you [Music] After that, the guide took us to another shop. There weren't many products left in the shop, and you can only buy the available products with ration cards. [Music] I my vegetarian. I let you in. >> In Cuba, there is a ration card shopping system called Libraa. This system, which has been in place since the 1960s, aims to provide basic food access to millions of people. However, due to the dire state of the economy, the food rations per household are shrinking in size and scope. According to Reuters, a Cuban citizen could buy 80 g of bread per day with the ration card in 2024, whereas now it is down to 60 g. Moreover, this rationing system does not meet the population's entire food needs. So, they must buy most of their food from peddlers or grocery stores. A young mother realized that we were filming and thought we were journalists. She told Chris about her economic hardship. [Applause] [Music] [Music] After we left, the guide told us about the black market for food and medicine, giving us a concrete example. >> Behind me is one of the secret stores here in Cuba. You will see many of these because they try to buy products from the outside and they sell them secretly because these are some products that the governments does not provide. So the people got to make their living. They come here, they buy groceries and they take them home. According to the locals, the government does not allow this type of of sales. Is not allowed. So if they catch them, they go to prison. So they do they do have do they have two options either die of eservation or either selling these products for the locals. We went down a street and met a man standing on the side of the road with a bag of medicine. He had obtained medicines that were unavailable inarmacies and was selling them on the black market. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. We were interested in what he was telling us. We went to a pharmacy pretending to be customers and tried to get information from the female pharmacist to see ifies really had medicine. [Music] From what the woman told us and from looking at the medicine cabinets, we understood that there is indeed a problem finding ing medicine in the country. Cuba is actually a highly developed country in terms of medicine, but it does not have the capacity to produce medicine for every disease. Therefore, they depend on foreign countries. Currently, they cannot access 70% of the medicines they need. So, patients must visit more than one pharmacy to find the medicines they require. What about the situation in hospitals in the country? To learn about health services in Cuban hospitals, we obtained permission from a hospital official to enter the hospital with a guide. Taking a few images there might give us an idea of the country's health system. First, we went to the cashier's desk and then we met with the woman in charge to request permission to take pictures. I don't know what country you live in, but Cuban hospitals seem calm and not overcrowded. The corridors are clean, and the atmosphere is spacious. The hospital building is old and needs repairs inside and out. [Music] In the emergency room, there is a cubicle for almost every patient, and attendants sit in chairs next to them. This is what a Cuban hospital looks like, and the doctors are trying to treat their patients with all the resources they have. After doing so much research in the capital city on the first day, our teammate Chris wanted to go to a barber shop for a shave. We walked around the streets of the capital looking for a barber shop. It might be a good idea to go to a barber shop. That way, we would have a chance to get information about Cuba from the barber. We finally found a barber and asked him a few questions. Barb, he earns 100 pesos or about $4 for a haircut. The 7,000 peso monthly shop rent he pays is about $300, which I think is expensive for Cuba. Rent in the capital is also expensive. For example, the monthly rent for a two-bedroom house in a tourist area is easily over $1,000 for a short term. Long-term rentals can be found for an average of around $600 per month. However, even if you pay this amount, you may not get the efficiency you expect from a house in Cuba. There are frequent power outages in the country. According to various news organizations, the number of power outages in Cuba has increased significantly since 2024. Some families have even started producing coal to meet some of their energy needs. Occasionally, there are mass power outages that can last up to 20 hours during the day. For example, we noticed that some of the side streets had poor lighting at night. Sometimes it is difficult to see in front of you because of the darkness. These visuals are the clearest indication of this issue. Similarly, internet speeds in the country are low and internet freedom is restricted. For instance, the concept of free journalism is not understood in this country and those who produce opposition content are suppressed. Anti-government online news is often censored as well. On our trip there, after leaving the barberh shop, we noticed a significant amount of garbage and debris on some streets. There are piles of garbage on some of the main streets and in the alleyways. Some houses in the neighborhood are uninhabitable and look like ruins. Construction equipment is trying to clean up the area, but the work is obviously slow. Trash is another problem here in Cuba. As you can see, there's so many trash across the streets. And there are some locals that say that even a month has to go through without people picking up the trash. We took bicycle taxis to get away and go to our hotel. In Cuba, there are bicycle taxis that run on pedal power. They don't have an engine. These taxis are powered by human muscle and are a logical transportation option for tourists traveling short distances in the city. Okay. The next day, the guide directed us toward a more interesting topic. In Cuba, there is a concept called ginoteras. Cuban women provide companionship services to wealthy men who visit the country. Don't think of this service only in sensual terms. In Cuba, you can hire a paid girlfriend. You can meet these women, go out to eat with them, have fun in bars and discos with them, and have sexual contact with them if you want. Some women do this on the side, and they are called ginoteras. They each have their own private lives in different professions. However, due to economic difficulties, they have paid friendships with tourists on the side. Our guide took us to the home of a young woman who does this and introduced us to her. There, we had a chance to talk to the woman and her mother. [Music] [Music] Rafael [Music] [Music] [Music] [Applause] [Music] [Music] It seems that this escort has to take care of her children and mother. Her neighbor showed us her ration book and told us about the lack of economic opportunities. [Music] Tourism is undoubtedly one of the biggest sources of income in Cuba. Last year, for example, 2.2 million tourists visited Cuba, earning $1.3 billion. However, this amount alone is insufficient for developing an entire country. Although bars, discos, tourist vehicle tours, and beaches add color to Cuba's tourism. The country needs more money. Additionally, some locals in Cuba provide tourists with negative experiences. They may scam them or provide poor service. The Cuban government must be aware of this because they have fined some locals for harassing tourists. You may be hearing about this type of punishment for the first time. Indeed, there is a penalty in Cuba for staying away from tourists. And we visited the home of someone who received this penalty. He cannot approach tourists within 500 meters. And he told us how he received this punishment. Okay. If you're a tourist going there to have fun, rest assured that Cuba's night life is lively all year round. The people of this country love to dance and listen to Latin music. While dancing, they forget their problems and have fun. Live music is a must at venues. Nevertheless, it is always useful to keep yourself and your valuables safe. You may unfortunately come across some extortion and pickpocketing incidents. In conclusion, Cuba is a country full of paradoxes. On the one hand, it offers socialist solidarity, cultural richness, and tropical charm. At the same time, it is a country characterized by harsh economic restrictions, political limitations, and chronic crisis. Despite all the difficulties, I think the joy and solidarity of the Cuban people will continue to be the greatest force that keeps Cuba going. If you like the video, please like it and subscribe to the channel. See you in the next video.

safetyNotes: I don't know what country you live in, but Cuban hospitals seem calm and not overcrowded
They don't have an engine
Don't think of this service only in sensual terms
They may scam them or provide poor service
Nevertheless, it is always useful to keep yourself and your valuables safe
You may unfortunately come across some extortion and pickpocketing incidents

videos.title: 7 Things No One Told Me About Havana Cuba
videos.description: 7 Things No One Told Me Before Visiting Cuba
videos.transcript: in this video I'm going to share with you guys seven things no one told me about Cuba before visiting because I was like man why did anyone tell me about this why did anyone tell me about that now I will say before I booked my trip I did a ton of research so some of these items I'm gonna share with you guys uh I knew beforehand but again it came from a ton of reading and going on many forums I also want to point out that some information that I received from people who visited Cuba previously in the past and what they told me is a little outdated some of the YouTube comments people wrote are outdated as well so again I'm just gonna share a few of these things with you and hopefully if you're planning a trip to Cuba what you learned in this video may help you the first thing no one told me is that you can actually use US Dollars previously what you heard and what you still may hear today is don't bring USD only bring Euros or maybe Canadian dollars meaning if you have of U.S currency exchange them for euros and then exchange the Euros for Cuban pesos now for me I did not want to do that I did not want to exchange twice and get hit with fees twice and then I wasn't in my home country so I wasn't able to just go to my bank and ask for euros so did some research and realized that you can use US Dollars you just can't use them in the official exchange offices which you don't want to do and I'll get into that in the next part but as far as buying items on the street like souvenirs you can use it with tips and Resorts from what I heard you can use it to pay taxi drivers or even in restaurants you can use US Dollars sometimes the check will come back to you it'll have two options to pay in Cuban pesos and also the amount in US Dollars that being said I do believe whenever you're in a foreign country you should use their currency but I'm just trying to make the point that you can bring US dollars to Cuba the second thing no one told me was about the informal or Street exchange rate for people who don't know in Cuba there's an official exchange rate and then there's an unofficial or informal exchange rate for anyone who's been to Argentina you know exactly what I'm talking about in Argentina if you go to the official exchange offices you'll get a certain rate then quote unquote on the street you're able to get double where in Cuba you're able to get three to four times more so for example the official rate was and I'm around up it was 25 Cuban pesos for one US Dollar on the quote-unquote street it was 100 pesos for one US dollar and when I say Street I mean basically anywhere outside of the official offices you will not have to go far to get this rate and most likely people will come up to you first once they figure out you're a foreigner so just keep that in mind this is another reason why I said you could bring US dollars because in the informal market and they accept it now if you plan on going to the official Exchange offices then you might as well take a trip to Switzerland or Sweden because you're going to be paying the same prices a bottle of water is probably going to cost you eight ten bucks a little can of juice or soda if you drink that it's gonna be like five six dollars a meal of beans and rice probably like twelve dollars so again just remember there are two rates the official rate and then the unofficial rate these next few items I'm gonna preface them by saying that I was putting off going to Cuba for multiple reasons I actually had a buddy who went in the past and he was telling me slaw you need to go it's an it's an experience and I told him two reasons why I'm not going one I need consistent internet I know some people say hey go to Cuba and disconnect and just put your phone down but no that's that's not me I need internet and the second reason was I believe previously there were only two ways only two types of accommodations you could reserve on the island one was an expensive hotel which I couldn't afford and two who was something that's called a casa particular which basically a fancy term for saying a room in someone's home which again I did not want to do that either thankfully it seems like things have changed and I'm going to touch on them when it comes to booking accommodations you do not have to stay at an expensive hotel nor do you have to stay in someone's home you could actually book a private place so that can be a studio one bedroom what have you when I was in vadodetto in a touristy place I had a whole like guest Suite to myself so at the kitchen and I think it was a two bedroom private entrance all that good stuff when I was in Havana I had a private Studio on the sixth floor great views hot water AC super clean I used Airbnb but I'm sure it's the same for other third-party sites there are filters that you can select and all you have to do is just check the filter that says entire Place meaning the entire place to yourself and Airbnb will only show those listings and again I'm sure it's the same for other accommodation sites now let's talk about the Internet situation which is a little it's interesting from what I understand from talking to people there are only two well there are two main ways to get internet in Cuba one staying at a hotel usually the more expensive route or two finding a public hot spot outside which are usually at parks you're not going to see Wi-Fi in any of the restaurants or the cafes that being said some accommodations have Wi-Fi in their Apartments the same way I talked about earlier basically when you're on let's say Airbnb choose the filter that says Wi-Fi it'll show you all the listings that supposedly have Wi-Fi I say supposedly because make sure to read the description and always read the reviews and see what they say another thing no one told me about Cuba before visiting is that they have SIM cards so just the same way if you travel to if you're traveling to a foreign country or even in your home country you're able to to purchase a SIM card and it has you know minutes uh I forgot what else talk Voice or whatever it's called and then the plan will offer you a certain amount of data the Cuban SIM cards are the same way I had one and then for about ten dollars I was able to get like 15 16 gigs of Internet which was perfect because then my options open when it comes to accommodations if I already know I'm gonna purchase the SIM card that has internet I may not need my accommodation to have it I will say the internet was actually it wasn't that bad it was fast enough for me to upload videos which is what I was doing when I was in Cuba watch YouTube surf the Internet etc etc and also it worked outside of uh Havana I went to Varadero and the internet still worked the bars went down a little bit but for the most part it got the job done next up something else no one told me about Cuba before visiting is that at least in Havana in the tourist area in the Central Area nothing's free not even information not even asking someone hey where is this place at and them walking you there that's not free either that caught me off guard because I've been to other countries where I was lost and I asked for directions and instead of pointing me there someone would walk with me to the place and they wouldn't ask for any money additionally when I arrived at the airport in Cuba I forgot that I couldn't exchange my US dollars at the official places because I just want to exchange a couple bucks so I can use the bus but I wasn't able to do that I spoke with a lady there she went all around the airport trying to help me out she was talking to some of the people in charge of the buses and she was just doing a lot eventually she found me a taxi a taxi driver who had enough change because I had a huge hundred dollar bill and at the end she didn't charge me anything so I had that mindset that it would be the same in the the tourist area but nope not the same and Havana Centro just keep in mind that again nothing's free Cubans are very friendly which throws you have a guard because sometimes you don't know someone's being friendly just cuz or if they're trying to get something out of you only two more to lists this next one no one told me that I wouldn't be able to use the supermarkets man now I don't know if foreigners can't use them at all but I do know they only accept credit card and my bank cards are all American and from what I heard you can't use them in Cuba they may get blocked so I didn't want to chance it and because of that oh man it was It was kind of rough I'm maybe like some of you guys I like munching on things like snacks and candies every now and then maybe some some chips I wasn't able to do that because I couldn't go to any of the the supermarkets maybe in other parts of the city they have supermarkets where you can pay in cash but from what I saw when I was down there that that wasn't the case I tried purchasing a few snacks here and there from some small places but oh man they they were bad I'm pretty sure they were spoiled and and old so so just know that you're not gonna do much much munching and snacking in Cuba and whatever items cosmetic items Pharmaceuticals bring them with you and last but not least I'm not gonna lie people told me about this and that's about the Cuban whoo the ladies if you're watching you can go ahead and click off the video I appreciate you let me talk to the fellas but the the Cuban ladies out there very lovely it reminded me of my time in Brazil where you have all spectrums as far as complexions also you have a lot of mixtures out there that creates like this exotic being I never seen so many like hazel eyes and light like Crystal eyes again it just reminded me of Brazil it was very the people were very exotic and one point I want to stress you're not gonna see a lot of plastic surgeries and slicing and dicings of the body nor are you going to see a lot of add-ons maybe because they don't have it in Cuba or it's super expensive to get I just didn't see a lot of that and so it's really what you see is what you get and I enjoyed that but with all that being said these are the seven things no one told me about Cuba before visiting I want to share these with you guys because I do think there's information out there but some of it may be outdated and just just wrong and I'm hoping these helped you especially if you're thinking about planning a trip here soon or maybe sometime in the future if you are watching this video a year from now things may have changed again so always double check and and verify so with that I'm ended right here I appreciate you guys watching the video I hope that has helped you hope you liked the video and I'll catch you guys at the next one deuces

videos.title: ONE DAY IN HAVANA CUBA - The Best Things to See and Do - 4K
videos.description:
We spent one full day exploring Cuba’s vibrant and historical capital, Havana. The capital is home to many historic monuments, colonial buildings, remnants of the revolution, and of course, classic 1950's cars. After spending time exploring Revolution Square, El Malecon, Old Havana, Fusterlandia and many more top sights, we’ve assembled a one day in Havana travel guide so you can see the highlights in one single day.

We hope you enjoy our Havana travel guide and find it useful! If there’s other places that you recommend checking out, be sure to let us know in the comments below. We’re planning on going back to Havana soon to explore other hidden areas of this wonderful city.

📽️ Watch our Varadero travel video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qaWfwTrr2GE&t=8s
 
💻 Follow us on social media:
https://www.facebook.com/toucantravellers/
https://www.instagram.com/toucan_travellers/
Pinterest - https://uk.pinterest.com/toucantravellers/
 
🔔 Subscribe to our channel: /  @ToucanTravellers 
 
🔔 Subscribe to our secondary channel for hotel guides and reviews:  /   @ToucanTravellersHotels 

📽️ Havana video chapters: 
0:00 Intro 
0:37 Fusterlandía 
1:15 Havana Capital Building 
1:33 Grand Theatre of Havana 
1:43 Central Park 
1:59 Ernest Hemmingway Bars
2:38 Old Square (Plaza Vieja) 
3:08 Plaza de Armas 
3:24 Plaza de Catedral 
3:32 St Francis of Asisi Square 
3:45 The real Havana 
4:12 Museum of the Revolution 
4:45 Revolution Square 
5:18 Classic car tour 
5:38 The Malecon 
6:03 Hotel Nacional de Cuba 
6:20 Fortresses of Morro and San Carlos de la Cabaña

#cuba #havana #havanatravel #travelcuba #cubatravelguide #lahabana
videos.transcript: cuba's capital havana better known as la havana in spanish is full of colour and culture after spending one full day exploring this vibrant city we've created an action-packed travel guide where we cover the top things to see and do havan is a popular place to visit if you're on a cruise or are coming from the popular beach town of valladelo we hope you enjoy our video [Music] before venturing into the center of havana ask your tour guide or taxi driver to stop off at the mesmerizing neighborhood attraction known as fostelandia this sprawling labyrinth of mosaic cover streets is an artwork of artist jose fuster who started the project in 1975 in an effort to improve his rundown fishing community jose gained inspiration from the famous architect gaudi and picasso which is reflected in his artwork at the park [Music] after exploring frustulandia venture into the heart of the city starting with old havana you'll see one of cuba's most iconic buildings el capitolio it was once the headquarters of cuban congress but is now an academy of science and technology [Music] right next to el capitolio is the picturesque grand theater which was built in 1914 this beautifully designed theater is currently home to the cuban national ballet [Music] across the road from both buildings central park is a nice place to relax in the shade after sightseeing in the sun enjoy a drink in a park bench and soak in the sights of this picturesque park [Music] after spending some time sightseeing you can grab a drink at ernest hemingway's old hangouts this world famous american novelist spent decades of his life living in cuba finding inspiration for many of his pieces of work in the bars and hotels of havana floridita bar was his go-to daiquiri bar whilst la bodakita del medio is where he enjoyed the mojito wow [Music] boasts some of the most impressive architecture in havana with rows of colorful colonial buildings alongside more modern designs take time to wander around the square taking pictures of the beautiful buildings and stop off by one of the many galleries if you have time [Music] other iconic havana squares to check out include plaza de armas which is havana's oldest and most historical plaza with statues and architecture dating back to the 1500s there's also plaza de la cathedral which is home to the most majestic church in cuba the cathedral has been visited by three popes and once held the remains of new world explorer christopher columbus finally walk onward to saint francis of assisi square for another impressive church in some beautiful buildings along the surrounding streets [Music] havana has a somewhat glamorous reputation however it has a side to it that is very different and is a real representation of how many locals live outside of the newly renovated and highly maintained squares buildings are falling apart and locals can't afford or find the supplies to maintain them streets have a much more rugged appearance and shops have completely empty shelves to see what life is really like we encourage you to head away from the tourist trail it's eye-opening and will give you an insight into other aspects of havana [Music] stories monuments and remnants of the cuban revolution can be found throughout cuba to gain an insight into this important historic event and get a better understanding of the country and its people we highly recommend a stop at the museum of the revolution [Music] revolution plaza is one of the largest public squares in the world it was a site of many important events during the cuban revolution and has since been transformed with an array of monuments and memorabilia top things to see here include the huge building murals of freedom fighters che guevara and camila segun fuegos and the jose marti memorial another thing we loved about this area was the cool rows of classic cars in the car park [Music] we personally recommend hiring a classic car with a driver for a couple of hours while super touristic and cliche it's an extremely fun way to see the sights of havana and you have the freedom to spend as much time as you like in each place [Music] one of the best places to check out on your classic car adventure is a legendary el malecon el malecon is havana's long seaside promenade and the famous meeting point for lovers locals hang out here on a weekend and friends sit down for a catch up throughout the day [Music] [Music] in the area visit the famous hotel nationale for a drink on the patio it has some amazing views of the malecon [Applause] [Music] it's been a long day of sightseeing so our final recommendation havana is castillo de los tres les del morro otherwise known as moro fortress it's a key fortress that was built to defend the city against pirates and invaders in the 1500s you'll also be able to see some incredible views of havana here we hope you enjoyed watching our one day havana travel guide if you liked our video and want to see more of cuba be sure to check out our other videos subscribe and click the bell notification for more holiday content hasta pronto

recommendations: ONE DAY IN HAVANA CUBA - The Best Things to See and Do - 4K
If there’s other places that you recommend checking out, be sure to let us know in the comments below

videos.transcript: Welcome to our Havana vlog, where we dive into the heart of Havana Cuba, showcasing the vibrant life, stunning architecture and rich culture of this iconic city. From the cobblestone streets of old Havana to the rhythmic sounds of salsa that fill the air. Join us as we explore the best of Havana, offering you an ultimate Havana travel guide packed with invaluable Havana travel tips. Whether you're planning your journey or just dreaming of Cuban adventures. This Havana, Cuba vlog is your gateway to discovering the magic of Havana. Don't miss out on any of our adventures. Make sure to hit that subscribe button to keep up with all our travels. Dive into the beauty and excitement of Havana with us, and let's explore the wonders of Havana, Cuba, together. Havana, the capital city of Cuba, is renowned for its rich cultural heritage, vibrant music scene and historical significance. It's famous for its beautifully preserved Spanish colonial architecture, especially in Old Havana. The UNESCO's World Heritage Site. The city is also known for its iconic vintage American cars that line the streets, offering a glimpse into its unique historical period. Havana's lively atmosphere is complemented by its famous music and dance, particularly salsa, which can be seen and heard in numerous clubs and bars across the city. Moreover, Havana is the birthplace of premium Cuban cigars, attracting aficionados from around the world to its famous cigar factories. Getting to Havana typically involves flying into Jose Marti International Airport, which serves as the main gateway for international travellers to Cuba. From major cities around the world, there are direct and connecting flights available with increased options from Latin American and European countries. Upon arrival, visitors can take a taxi, a shuttle bus, or arrange for private transportation to reach the city centre, which is about 30 minutes away from the airport. For those travelling from the United States, Direct flights are available but may come with certain restrictions, so it's essential to check the latest travel advisories. Additionally, some travellers choose to arrive in Havana via cruise ships which dock at the terminal Sierra maestra in Old Havana. Traveling around Havana is an adventure in itself, with several options available to visitors. The most iconic way to explore the city is by riding in a classic American vintage car, which can be hired as a taxi for a unique sightseeing experience. For short distances, walking is an excellent way to appreciate the city's architecture and vibrant street life. Public buses, known as “guaguas,” are a cheap way to get around, but they can be crowded and confusing for first-time visitors. Alternatively, bicycle taxis and small motorbike taxis called “coco-taxis” offer convenient and affordable transportation for navigating the city's narrow streets. For a more comfortable experience, regular taxis and ride-sharing services are also available. In Havana, the must-visit area is Old Havana (Habana Vieja), with its historic buildings, cobblestone streets, and lively plazas such as Plaza de la Catedral and Plaza Vieja. The Malecón, Havana's famous seaside promenade, offers stunning ocean views and is a popular spot for both locals and tourists, especially at sunset. The Castillo de la Real Fuerza and the Fortaleza de San Carlos de la Cabaña are impressive fortifications that provide insight into Havana's military history. The Gran Teatro de La Habana and the Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes showcase Cuba's cultural and artistic contributions. Finally, the Revolution Square (Plaza de la Revolución) is an iconic landmark where major political rallies have taken place and features monuments dedicated to Cuban heroes like José Martí. Exploring the vibrant streets of Old Havana is a must, where you can immerse yourself in the city's rich history and colonial architecture. Taking a ride in a classic American car offers a nostalgic tour of Havana's iconic landmarks and neighborhoods. For culture enthusiasts, attending a live salsa music performance or taking a salsa dance lesson provides an authentic Cuban experience. Art lovers should not miss the chance to visit Fábrica de Arte Cubano, a unique fusion of art gallery and nightclub. Additionally, a visit to a cigar factory, such as the Real Fábrica de Tabacos Partagás, offers insight into the art of cigar making, a cornerstone of Cuban heritage. The best time to visit Havana is between November and April, during the dry season, when the weather is pleasant with low humidity and cooler temperatures. This period avoids the rainy season and the potential hurricanes from June to October, ensuring more comfortable exploration of the city. The cooler months also coincide with several cultural events and festivals offering a richer experience of Havana's vibrant culture. December and January in particular see a spike in tourist activity due to the festive season and New Year celebrations. Early spring before the onset of the wet season is ideal for enjoying Havana's outdoor attractions without the crowds of peak winter months. Hotel prices in Havana can vary widely, with options ranging from budget friendly causes, particularly private homes to luxury hotels. Budget accommodations might start from as little as $20 to $30 per night, while mid-range hotels can cost between $50 to $100 per night. Luxury hotels in prime locations can exceed $200 per night. Food prices in Havana also offer a range with street food and local eateries providing meals for as low as $2 to $5. Dining in mid-range restaurants can cost between $10 to $20 per person, while high end dining experiences can be $30 to $50 per person excluding drinks. Ropa Vieja, a flavorful stew made of shredded beef in a tomato sauce base, is a classic Cuban dish that is a must-try in Havana. For a taste of traditional Cuban street food, sample Tostones, which are twice-fried plantain slices, often served with garlic sauce. A Cuban sandwich, made with ham, roasted pork, Swiss cheese, pickles, and mustard on Cuban bread, offers a delicious quick meal. For seafood lovers, Grilled Lobster is widely available and showcases the island's fresh seafood offerings. No culinary exploration in Havana would be complete without trying a Mojito or Daiquiri, classic Cuban cocktails that perfectly complement the local cuisine. When in Havana, purchasing authentic Cuban cigars is a top choice for many visitors, offering a taste of the country's renowned tobacco craftsmanship. Cuban rum is another must-buy, celebrated for its quality and flavor, perfect for savoring the local spirit or as a gift. Local art and handicrafts found in markets like the Almacenes San José Artisans’ Market provide unique souvenirs and support local artisans. Traditional Cuban music CDs or vinyl records make for a great way to bring back the sounds of salsa, son, and rumba. Additionally, buying a classic Guayabera shirt offers a wearable piece of Cuban culture, ideal for the tropical climate. Old Havana Habana Vieja is the best area for travellers looking to immerse themselves in the city's rich history and vibrant street life, with accommodations ranging from luxury hotels to charming cars is particularly. Vedado offers a more modern experience with plenty of hotels, nightlife and restaurants suitable for those who prefer a contemporary urban atmosphere. Miramar, a more upscale district, provides luxurious hotels and rental properties ideal for travellers seeking tranquillity and exclusivity. Centro Havana is a good option for budget conscious travellers offering affordable lodging close to major attractions. For a more authentic experience. Staying in a CASA particular private home allows for personal interaction with Cuban families and insight into local life. Havana is generally considered safe for tourists with a relatively low crime rate compared to other major cities. The Cuban government prioritises tourism, safety and incidents involving tourists. A rare. However, like in any urban area, it's important to take standard precautions, such as avoiding unlit streets at night and keeping valuables secure. Petty theft and pickpocketing can occur in crowded areas, so staying vigilant in tourist hotspots is advised. Engaging with licensed taxis and official tour operators also contributes to a secure visit. Havana offers a unique blend of cultural, historical and sensory experiences, making it a worthwhile destination for many travellers. The city's rich history is palpable in its architecture and museums, while its vibrant music and dance scene provide a lively cultural immersion. Havana's culinary offerings from street food to high end dining showcase the diversity of Cuban cuisine. The warmth and hospitality of its people add to the city's charm, making visitors feel welcome. For those interested in experiencing a city that embodies resilience and tradition while embracing change, Havana is undoubtedly worth a visit. As our journey through Havana, Cuba, comes to an end. We hope you've enjoyed exploring the vibrant streets, rich history and lively culture alongside this in this Havana vote. From the unforgettable sights of old Havana to the invaluable travel tips we've shared, our adventure has been a deep dive into the heart of Cuban life. If this Havana travel guide has inspired your wanderlust or helps you plan your next trip, don't forget to hit that subscribe button for more travel tips and adventures. Thank you for joining us on this unforgettable Havana, Cuba vlog journey. Until next time, keep exploring and remember, the spirit of Havana is just a play away.

videos.title: Havana is NOT dangerous but Keep your Eyes open: What your Guide Book Doesn't Tell you
videos.description:
Cuba is one of the craziest places I've ever visited and it has earned a special place in my heart. Stunning colonial buildings stand side-to-side with crumbling modern buildings, a sign that times are slowly changing and perhaps not for the best. Trapped in a time warp and reeling from an economic embargo that has grated for more than half a century, this is a country where you can wave goodbye to everyday assumptions and expect the unexpected.

In this video I will take you on a tour of Old Havana and give you some helpful tips to make the most of your time in Cuba.
